channeling-your-inner-child-chasing:
i started this draft in 2024. finishing it now as something more personal.

i got invited to give a talk at a summer camp for kids, shout out to Oreofe for that. i had a whole product design talk ready. then i got introduced as an engineer. a whole GMNSE. and just like that, the plan changed. i had to pause, scrap everything, and give an impromptu speech on the spot.

what followed was a room full of kids telling me what they wanted to be when they grew up. doctors, lawyers, pilots, engineers. you name it. and somewhere in that room, listening to all of them, i started thinking about my own list. the one i had at their age. the one i quietly stopped talking about.

that talk eventually became a lightning talk at work. and now it’s this.

Hi, I’m Adedayo. former aspiring pilot, former aspiring F1 driver, never aspired to be a doctor 💀, ex mechanical engineer, currently a ux designer & fitness bro, lowkey training for an ironman, aspiring chef, aspiring porsche owner, etc.

i remember Joseph, one of my earliest friends from as far back as i can remember. we did almost everything together, competed in everything in primary school, and he was one of the first people to show me how to flex my biceps, even with how tiny they were. he said he wanted to be a lawyer when he grew up. i hope he’s good and the universe is kind to him wherever he is now.

the kids in that room had their lists. i had mine too. i wanted to be a pilot. i wanted to be a chef. Tobi and I had already decided we’d be engineers purely because of our love for cars. best bet we ended up as engineers but neither of us practices. life. lmaoo.

those weren’t childish fantasies. they were just... us. undisturbed. no bills, no “be realistic”, no timeline. just the thing we wanted and the certainty that we’d get there.

i used to race Tobi as a kid. we’d go until we were both completely spent. we still have a speedy joke we crack till today. i even represented red house and got medals. somewhere along the way i stopped. maybe, just maybe, that’s not the end of that story. i’ve tracked about 335km of running since i started, almost a year in. if i wanted medals as an adult, why not?

first time i was in a pool i was around 6 or 7. me, Tobi, Jinmi and Jeminipe. went for classes once and that was it. scared the whole time. the lessons stuck but i was a child, and coming back to the pool as an adult is genuinely one of the hardest things i’ve done. now i’m training to swim laps. what kills me is when people ask if i swim professionally and i just say “not yet.” thanks to all my coaches.

side quest on the list: sign up for an ironman. or at least a half. we’ll see.

but look at me, you, us now. trying to navigate adulthood while still figuring out how to grow up.

my flare for cooking has never faded. i still intend to attend culinary school. is it the conventional timing? no. does that matter? not at all. would i still do it? yes, eventually. especially after watching The Bear.

growing up, i used to look at planes in the sky and think of my grandma. she was always in the air and i’d think, it wouldn’t be bad to fly one of those. then the Sosoliso crash happened. more crashes followed on the news. and my head went. nope. even though we lived right by the airport. close enough that i could always see the planes coming in to land.

one very close call i ever had was one dry, dusty harmattan morning. for those who don’t know harmattan, imagine the driest, harshest weather you’ve ever felt. skip the vaseline and you’re going to be ashy. i was on my way back from school when i heard it. planes are supposed to be close to the tarmac at that point, sure. but this one was directly over my father’s house. imagine looking up and seeing the underside of a plane so close that if you jumped high enough, you could touch it. it circled back and landed safely. but reflecting on that moment made me appreciate what it actually takes to be a pilot. people’s lives are literally in your hands. respect to them fr.

been in here once. might as well run it back.

here’s the thing nobody really says out loud. we don’t give up on our dreams because we forget them. we give up because we get tired of defending them. tired of the “are you sure?” and the “is that realistic?” and the quiet version of both that lives in our own heads.

fear of failure hits different as an adult. as a kid you’d fall, dust yourself off, and go again without a second thought. i have a theory about this actually. when a toddler falls, they won’t cry unless it’s genuinely that painful. but the moment the adults around them rush over saying sorry? that’s when the waterworks roll in. somewhere along the way we became the adults in our own story. the falling started to mean something. started to feel like proof of something.

then there’s the social noise. “what will people think?” becomes a background track you can’t turn off. and underneath all of it, the quietest one: i’m too old. it’s too late. i missed it.

you didn’t.

your dreams don’t have an expiration date. the spark you felt as a kid, whether it was planes or kitchens or racetracks, that wasn’t a phase. that was a signal. and over the years i’ve met so many people living out exactly what you’re reading right now, without ever putting a name to it.

most of us say we just want to be kids again with no worries. but we all know that’s not really it. the reason we say it is the same reason we haven’t fully let go. we still want the thing.

career pivots are more common now than they’ve ever been. that’s not me being motivational, that’s just true. people are switching lanes at 25, 40, 60. the path doesn’t have to look the way you imagined it at 8 years old. it just has to be moving toward something real.

start part-time. take the class. tell one person about the thing you’ve been sitting on. break it down until it stops feeling impossible. the dream doesn’t have to arrive all at once.

and Dami said it better than i could:

there’s a theory i came across recently from ’s post that stuck with me. the river theory. the idea that life moves like a current. you have a boat, you have a paddle, you can steer. but you cannot control the river itself. you don’t choose where the rocks rise or when the current shifts. and yet the river keeps moving.

your childhood dreams are still in the water. the path just took a few bends you didn’t draw on the map.

and honestly, the crux of all of this is simple. you can just do things. especially the things you said you’d do when you were a kid, no matter how silly or funny it sounds now. don’t be surprised if i pop out as a pilot at some point. i’ve been in the cockpit once. might as well run it back.

your dreams haven’t expired. they’ve just been waiting for you to stop making excuses and start. the path looks different than you drew it back then. that’s fine. start anyway.

leaving you with a quote from a book my sister got me for my 18th.

Even as we dream, we begin to succeed..
Even as we succeed, we begin to dream again

Adedayo 𓂀
for-my-27th-i-write-to-my-mum:
Dear Aderonke,

I hope you’re resting well.

I started writing this at your 10th year remembrance but I never got to finish it. I still couldn’t find the words. And then I lost one ear of the buds Fisayo got me, and something about losing a thing you love so quietly broke me open again.

I once read somewhere that we don’t look for old songs; we look for the memories they carry.

I was playing No Blame one morning when I heard the line, “I was seven years old, waiting on you in pyjamas,” and just like that, you came back to me.

And now, I hope you read this with the warm, big heart of love you’ve always loved me with.

I remember coming back from Ile-loun one Monday morning, running home for you to prep me for school, when I saw Diwura, one of my favourite neighbours, on his way out, reading a letter. I ran up to him, but he just looked up once and said his brother was dead. Then kept walking. The letter was the last thing he ever got from him.

I remember walking home slowly, still not believing it. And when I told you, you knelt down to my height, wiped the corner of your eye, held me, told me sorry, and then prepped me for school.

Maybe you knew.

I remember the first time I ever read the clock, calling out numbers that didn’t exist. Fisayo read it correctly. And just like that, you sat with me and taught me how.

Maybe you knew.

I remember sitting on Brosie and Edward’s backs while they did press-ups on Saturday cleaning days, and how they’d stop the ceiling fans mid-spin with their bare palms. You always pulled me away from all of that.

Maybe you knew.

I remember you getting me new batteries for my light-up running shoes to go with my red house sportswear. One had stopped working, and I was going to represent my house in the singles and relay races.

Maybe you knew.

I remember growing my wisdom tooth in JS1, terrible pain, and I couldn’t eat anything. You still found ways to feed me. I came home one day and told you I’d gone to get roasted yam despite the pain, and I watched your eyes light up as you held me.

Maybe you knew.

I remember waking up some nights with my nose burning and eyes watery from the aboniki you’d rubbed on my chest while I slept. You always said it was so I’d never get cold. Even Grandma still does the same thing when it rains.

Maybe you knew.

I remember coming back from Lagos one night with Big Daddy and Daddy Reni. When we got to the junction, I told them to drop me off, I was going home to you. They had to call you just to convince me to follow them. You told me they’d take care of me.

Maybe you knew.

I remember the first time I ever spoke Yoruba, my siblings laughed. You held me closer, corrected my pronunciation, and told them to leave your baby alone.

Maybe you knew.

I remember the day we made rolls in the kitchen with butter and I couldn’t believe my tongue. I’ve known my way around a kitchen ever since.

Maybe you knew.

I might not remember my first birthday, but there’s pictorial evidence of it. I remember my 8th or 9th, though. No one came around, I was angry and throwing a fit, and you were gone all day, but you came home late that night with a white square cake, told me sorry, gathered the neighbour’s kids, and made sure I was smiling in every picture as you sang me happy birthday.

Maybe you knew.

I remember staying up waiting for you one night when a storm hit and you couldn’t make it home. You called and told me to lock the doors, not to worry, that you’d be back soon. I fell asleep hugging a pillow in the same position you slept in on the bed. You came back in the morning with goodies.

Maybe you knew.

I remember the last time I hugged you. You told me you loved me and I hugged you a little tighter.

Maybe you knew.

I remember squeezing your hands while you slept on your sick bed. I should have held them a little longer.

This I know.

And I remember the last day I saw you.

I remember not being able to bring myself to post or write anything. I still couldn’t process it. wym RIP Mum?

I remember holding Brosie’s hand that night, mid-tears, and whispering to him that we’d be okay.

This I know.

I remember Mr Akinola hugging me in the car park on resumption day in SS1. I still didn’t let a single tear drop.

I remember being genuinely sad whenever I didn’t make the commendation list in senior school. What would you have said? But Fisayo told me to work for the final one. I cried myself to sleep some nights while prepping for my WAEC because I missed you so much, but I finished in the top 10. I hope you saw that. It made me happy.

I remember bawling my eyes out for three nights when Big Mummy Twins passed. wym your sister, your namesake, left me too. She always called to wish me a happy birthday. I hope you’re both resting in the same room the way you did back in school, and that you’re both wishing Itunu and me a happy birthday the way you would have if you were still here.

I remember writing my dedication section in my final year project and saying this page isn’t enough for you.

Maybe you knew you wouldn’t always be here to sing me happy birthday or teach me new things. So you did everything you could while you were here. Every single thing.

13 years now, and I still haven’t found all the words to tell you how much I love you and miss you. I’ll always be your baby.

People always said to say something at your burial, at your anniversary, on your posthumous birthdays. I never quite could.

I used to count the years growing up. You’d be retiring right around when I finished uni, Pops would be back in Ibadan, and everything would have been wonderful. But everything happens for a reason.

Still, let me tell you the good things. On your 10th anniversary, Fisayo got married. Brosie just got married too. And this you won’t believe, Edward drives Pops now. I brought my certificate home and took it to your grave, just like I used to bring home my report card in primary school. I hope it made you smile. Oh, and Kabiyeesi calls me Modakeke. And honestly, everyone still takes care of me because of you.

I still make your man smile. I don’t know if I do it on purpose. I think it’s just the way you loved me rubbing off on how I love him. But when I see it, I think of you.

I remember standing in the kitchen watching you and Pops make pounded yam together when I was younger. I didn’t know then that I was watching something I’d want to hold onto. Recently, Pops and I made it together. Just the two of us. I pounded and didn’t say much, but I think we both knew what we were really doing in that kitchen.

Maybe you knew.

Grandma sang my name the other day on Mother’s Day. Just like that, out of nowhere. And I knew. I knew that was you calling me. Especially after I wrote this to you. I held her a little longer than usual because I could hold you through her. Thank you.

I’m dedicating this day to you, my mother. Thank you for giving me life. For giving me wisdom. For giving me everything I know. For shaping me into who I am today.

And today, I know you’ve already told me a happy birthday somewhere. I just also wanted to tell you how much I genuinely miss you.

Keep resting, Iya Tayo.

Your Last Born, Adedayo.
you-can-just-do-things-i-did:
I wrote this at 4am in my mum’s hometown, and somehow it explains my entire 2025.

My last post ended with a simple idea: you can just do things.

Interestingly, I am writing this very first of many substacks at a late hour. Very late. It is 4 am somewhere in Ogun State, and I am putting together a reflection I promised myself I would write earlier.

But the timing feels right.

Looking back at 2025

2025 was a year of lessons. Every single thing carried one.

I did not tick everything off my list. But man proposes, and God disposes. You already know the saying.

What I did do was move.

End of year reflections made me realise something important. I did a lot more than I thought. I just did not notice while I was in it.

And somehow, I did not end the year burnt out like I usually do.

Instead, I felt ready. Energised. Clearer.

It made me realise how much energy I had been losing to things that were quietly holding me back.

Proof that things were actually done

Here is what movement looked like in real life:

I started running last year, and so far, I ran 174.1km, had a goal of 250km and found a pace only to realize people are insane - cos why’s your pace 3:12 fgs.
Anyways, this year, we push for my first 10k and maybe a half-marathon while at it.

I started swimming actively and fell in love with it.
I am still learning. If you think I swim well from the videos, that’s propaganda. I can be way better, and that is a 2026 goal. I want to swim laps without gassing. Proper laps.

The gym stayed light. No new PRs this year because recovery mattered more.
I learned this the hard way after messing up my back early in the year and dealing with pain for months. We fixed it, thankfully.

I sat in MMA classes and plan to start training fully in Q1 - I just need to prep myself mentally for the pain.

I started growing my hair again after five years of low cuts, then braided it. Definitely doing more of this - but wym i can’t pour water on my head.

I finally got my driver’s license after procrastinating for six years. Insane behaviour, honestly.

I started posting on LinkedIn & Tiktok as a test run.

I got commissioned to lead and work on multiple projects. Updates will come in Q2.

The biggest move of all was quitting my job after two years and five months.
We thank God for provision. He has been good.

I applied to many things and got rejected many times. Denied over and over again. Painful, yes. But all it takes is one yes.

This year, I am saying yes more.

Enter 2026

2026 feels different.

Not louder.
Not busier.
Just clearer.

This is the year where you do more and everything aligns.

You have free will.

Be at war with your vices, at peace with your neighbors, and let every New Year find you a better man.
— Benjamin Franklin

Before anything else, do not spread yourself too thin.
Shoutout to my mentor for constantly reminding me of this. She is amazing.

The real goal

The goal this year is simple.

Be a better YOU today than yesterday.
That means being intentional about the things you aim to do.

Doing more, but aligned

In 2026, I want more in the areas that matter:

Career, Finances. Relationships, Fitness, Exposure and many others i can’t begin to imagine

Not scattered.
Not forced.

Aligned.

2025 taught me that you really can just do things.
2026 is about choosing what is worth continuing.

And this time, I am moving with intention.

Adedayo

This piece was written slowly, in fragments, across time zones and headspaces. Some parts were uncomfortable to admit. Others felt obvious only in hindsight.

If 2025 taught me anything, it is that movement creates clarity, not the other way around.
